name: Periferia
classification_notes: Classified as Display by the foundry; letterforms built from a limited set of modular stamped forms with textural imperfections that give a hand-rendered, urban quality quite distinct from conventional type categories.
summary: A modular display typeface built from rubber-stamp forms, drawing on the rhythms and textures of the urban environment.
description: Each letterform is constructed from a limited set of stamped modules, and the textural irregularities of that process carry forward into the final design, giving it a hand-made quality rooted in physical mark-making. The foundry draws a deliberate parallel between rubber-stamping and movable type, framing Periferia as both a display face and a meditation on print history. Its Latin character set covers a broad range of European languages, making it serviceable for identity and editorial work across multiple markets.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Periferia as a display typeface that references the rhythm and pattern of the urban environment, developed using a limited set of modular stamped forms. The textural imperfections of the stamping process contribute a hand-rendered sensibility to the design, and the foundry celebrate the parallels between rubber-stamping and the tradition of movable type printing.

name: Friedel
classification_notes: Explicitly described as a neo-grotesque, monoline sans serif; derived from the 1965 VGC Solitaire specimen by John W Denzler, placing it firmly in the mid-century grotesque tradition.
summary: A neo-grotesque sans derived from a 1965 VGC Solitaire specimen, offering three weights with obliques and a generous x-height suited to both body and headline use.
description: Drawn from John W Denzler's Solitaire, originally published by Visual Graphics Corporation in 1965, Friedel takes the phototype era's monoline forms and gives them a large x-height that reads clearly at text and display sizes. Three weights run from Regular to Bold, each with a matching oblique, covering a broad range of Latin-script languages. The typeface has been used in projects for the Whitney Museum of American Art and Riposte magazine, demonstrating a practical range across cultural and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Friedel as a neo-grotesque, monoline sans with a generous x-height that gives body and headline text a clear, open character. Its starting point was a 1965 specimen of Solitaire by John W Denzler, published by Visual Graphics Corporation. The typeface is named after Murray Friedel, inventor of the PhotoTypositor, which VGC manufactured and marketed. Available in three weights across six styles, it can also be modified or tailored for specific project requirements.

name: Certeau
classification_notes: Certeau draws on geometric Modernist monoline traditions alongside 1930s Dutch and German sans-serif typography, placing it firmly in the geometric sans category, though its calligraphic lowercase details give it an idiosyncratic, humanist inflection.
summary: A geometric sans with calligraphic lowercase details, drawing on Modernist monoline typefaces and 1930s Dutch and German sans-serif traditions.
description: Three weights with matching obliques make up this six-style family, which balances rational geometric construction against idiosyncratic calligraphic details in select lowercase letterforms. The design research behind it sought typefaces that sit between order and personality, a tension visible in the contrast between its clean uppercase and more expressive lowercase. It has found use in brand identity and editorial contexts, including the Peirene Press identity and skincare brand Everyday Oil.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Certeau as influenced by geometric Modernist monoline typefaces and 1930s Dutch and German sans-serif typography, with expressive calligraphic details in certain lowercase letterforms. Available in three weights across six styles, it evolved through research into type styles that balance rationality with idiosyncrasy. The foundry also offers tailored modifications of Certeau for bespoke project requirements.

name: Remnants
classification_notes: Derived from Cyrillic letterforms found on an old Serbian book cover, expanded into a Latin character set with elongated monoline serifs. Its condensed proportions and ornamental quality place it firmly in the display category.
summary: A condensed display serif drawn from lettering found on an old Serbian book cover, expanded into a full Latin character set with elongated monoline serifs.
description: Originating from a display type found on a Serbian book cover, the design was built out into a complete Latin character set while preserving something of the visual character of Cyrillic letterforms. The elongated monoline serifs give it a distinctive, slightly archaic elegance suited to larger sizes. It has been used in record sleeve design and cultural institution merchandise, suggesting a natural fit for arts and editorial contexts.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Remnants as a typeface developed from a display type found on an old Serbian book cover, expanded into a full Latin character set while retaining the beauty of the Cyrillic alphabet. Condensed in style, it is intended for use at larger display sizes and is characterised by elongated monoline serifs.

name: Piloti
classification_notes: A light sans serif with tapered strokes drawn from stone-carved letterforms, high x-height and softened curves place it firmly in the humanist sans tradition.
summary: A light humanist sans with tapered, flared stems drawn from stone-carved letterforms, balancing warmth with graphic precision.
description: Piloti takes its structural cues from stone-carved lettering, translating those qualities into tapered strokes that flare at the ends and are cut perpendicular to the stem. A high x-height and softly curved forms give it a warmth that suits editorial, packaging and cultural institution work. Available in Regular and Regular Oblique, it is a restrained, single-weight family with broad Latin language support.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Piloti as a light sans serif with tapered stroke detailing inspired by stone-carved letterforms. Its high x-height and curved forms contribute softness and warmth, while the tapered stems flare at their ends and are cut perpendicular to the stem, lending character to the diagonal strokes. The name refers to columns of iron, steel or reinforced concrete that support a building above an open ground level.

name: Lining
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a decorative neo-grotesque sans-serif, with squared angles on certain letterforms giving it a mechanical, digital character despite its nineteenth-century origins.
summary: A decorative neo-grotesque revival drawing from an 1897 American Type Founders typeface, distinguished by squared angles that lend it a mechanical edge.
description: Rooted in a typeface family first advertised by the American Type Founders Company in 1897, Lining reinterprets its source material through squared letterform angles that read as distinctly digital despite the nineteenth-century origin. The family is minimal in scope, offering Regular and Regular Oblique only, but the character of individual forms makes it suited to poster and editorial contexts where a single weight can carry weight. The squaring of certain angles is the defining design decision, pulling the face away from conventional neo-grotesque softness into something more mechanical.
foundry_blurb: A Practice for Everyday Life describe Lining as a decorative neo-grotesque sans-serif developed as a contemporary reinterpretation of a typeface family first advertised by the American Type Founders Company in 1897. The squared angles of certain letterforms give it a digital character that belies its nineteenth-century origin, hinting at a mechanical influence upon its design.
